# Brief: Meg (PERSONA-011)
## Upcoming Meeting: 2026-04-07

## Since Last Meeting

No inter-meeting evidence was received between April 2 and April 7. The position heading into tonight is unchanged from what Meg relayed after the April 2 board meeting: the FY27 budget failed 5-2, Kaler closure and Option A reconfiguration are authorized, 78 positions are proposed for elimination, and an unconfirmed $300K–$1.05M in state funding was mentioned mid-meeting without being incorporated into any official figures. The board deferred adoption explicitly because of that pending state information, with tonight as the hard deadline.

That gap is just a gap — five days between a board deferral and a mandatory council presentation. There is nothing to read into it.

---

## Open Questions

These are the threads Meg is carrying in:

- **Which deficit number is the district presenting tonight — $7.2M or $8.4M?** I've been sourcing $7.2M since December. Councilor Matthews put $8.4M on the council record on March 19. If I post the wrong one tonight, I'll have to walk it back in front of everyone.
- **Is the state money real yet?** The union said $300K to $1.05M was coming from Augusta lobbying. If the superintendent cites it tonight, I need to know whether it's confirmed funding or still "anticipated" — those are two completely different things to post.
- **Are we going to a June 9 referendum on a budget the school board never actually voted yes on?** If yes, I need to explain what that means — and I'm not sure I fully understand the process yet.
- **What does the path from tonight to May 5 actually look like?** The board deferred. The council is doing their workshops. When does the school board vote again, and does a council workshop count as a substitute?
- **When are Kaler families getting the attendance boundary information?** Parents at Kaler are texting me directly. I don't have an answer and I don't want to guess.
- **What actually changed with the DEI director position?** The March 30 meeting had a last-minute restructuring that added no budget savings but removed the position's occupant. I need to be able to describe that accurately without overstating what happened.
- **Are the 72 pink slips and the 78 positions the same thing or not?** I've been holding back from explaining this discrepancy because I'm not confident I have it right.

## Agenda Implications

The April 7 agenda is long, but for Meg's purposes almost everything resolves into one item with a few notes around the edges.

---

**G.2 — Budget Presentation and Public Hearing (City Manager)**

This is the whole meeting. The city manager's position paper embeds the budget calendar Meg has been waiting for:

- **April 7:** Presentation and Public Hearing on Budget *(tonight)*
- **April 14 – Budget Workshop #1:** School department leads the list — followed by Police, Fire, Library, and others
- **April 28 – Budget Workshop #2:** Administrative and support departments
- **May 5:** Public Hearing / Approval of School Budget to Send to Voters
- **May 12:** Budget Workshop #3 (Parking Lot Items)
- **June 9:** School Budget Referendum
- **June 16:** Appropriation Resolve

These are the dates Meg's networks have been asking about. The June 9 referendum date is the most important single fact she can post tonight — it gives every parent in her group chats a deadline to care about.

**What to listen for:**
- Which deficit figure appears in the superintendent's presentation. If it's $7.2M, the Matthews discrepancy remains unresolved. If it's $8.4M, something changed and the district hasn't said so publicly.
- Whether the unconfirmed state funding is cited, and how — "we expect," "we have received," or "pending." The framing matters for what Meg can accurately relay.
- Whether any council member raises the fact that the school board did not adopt the budget before bringing it to the council tonight. If they do, Meg needs to capture that exchange — it's the kind of procedural wrinkle her networks will ask her about.
- Whether the presentation lists specific school buildings or only aggregate impacts. Meg's networks are sorted by school. Aggregate numbers don't answer "what does this mean for my kid at Skillin."
- Whether council members signal support for the 6% tax increase, push for less, or introduce any new figure. The tax ceiling is not locked until June 9.
- Matthews specifically. He put the $8.4M figure on the record last time. If he asks a question tonight that implies a different number than what the district presents, that's a second on-the-record discrepancy — and Meg will want to capture his exact words.

**Questions to prepare:**
- Does the presentation document reference the March 30 budget failure and explain why the board is presenting without an adopted vote?
- Is there a slide or page number she can cite when posting the 6% / $257 / year figure?
- Does the presentation specify which budget workshops will be open to public comment?

---

**H.1 — Postponed Order (ORDER #135-24/25 / ORDER #157-25/26), twice deferred from March 3 and March 19**

The agenda text doesn't name this item. It has been postponed twice — once from March 3 and once from March 19. The cumulative state identifies a Texas Instruments TIF 20-year extension as an open question, and the timing of these deferrals is consistent with that item.

If this is the TI TIF extension, it matters to Meg's networks as background context: locking in $1.2M per year in diverted property tax revenue for 20 years is part of the same structural picture as the school funding gap. She doesn't need to lead with it tonight, but if it passes quietly, she should note it in a follow-up post explaining why the district's funding problem isn't solved by one budget cycle.

**What to listen for:** The order number on the screen and any staff description of the subject matter. If the city manager's introduction names TIF, TI, or "tax increment financing," Meg should flag it.

**H.7 — Tax Abatement**

Abatements reduce the assessed tax base. If the abatement amount is substantial, it compounds the revenue-side pressure on the school budget. Meg should note the order number and amount if cited — worth a brief mention if it's a large figure, not otherwise.

---

**H.8 — PPLC License Renewal Comment (Maine DEP)**

Environmental / utility item. Not relevant to Meg's immediate focus.

---

**Public Comment (multiple points in the agenda)**

Public comment is often where the meeting's most quotable moments happen. Meg should be ready to note names and exact phrasing from anyone who speaks about the school budget — teachers, parents, union representatives. If someone cites a number she hasn't heard before, she should capture it and hold it rather than posting immediately. Unverified figures from public comment are the most common source of errors in meeting recaps.

---

## Watch For

- **The deficit number in the superintendent's presentation.** The moment the deficit figure appears on screen, screenshot it. If it's neither $7.2M nor $8.4M, that's a third number and she needs to note where it comes from.
- **Any language distinguishing "confirmed" from "anticipated" state funding.** The April 2 board meeting left the $300K–$1.05M range unconfirmed. If the superintendent presents it as confirmed tonight, that's the lead. If it's still pending, the budget math is still the same as it was.
- **Whether the council asks how the board can present an unadopted budget.** If a council member raises this and the superintendent or board chair answers, Meg needs that exchange — it's the procedural question her networks will ask first.
- **The June 9 referendum date, stated on the record.** This is the most actionable single fact for group chat. The moment a council member or staff person confirms June 9, that's the first post she can queue.
- **Matthews.** He has put a different deficit number on the record than the district's official figure. If he asks a question tonight, she should capture his exact wording, not paraphrase.
- **April 14 school workshop scope.** The agenda lists "School" as the first topic in Budget Workshop #1 on April 14. If any council member or staff member describes what specifically will be workshopped at that session, Meg has a preview to share.
- **Whether the council indicates any openness to supplementing the school budget above the 6% ceiling.** The April 2 meeting left open the question of whether confirmed state funding could change the adopted tax rate. If a council member signals flexibility, that's significant — if they signal the 6% ceiling is fixed regardless, that also matters.
- **H.1 reveal.** When the council reaches H.1, listen for the subject description in the staff introduction. If it involves TIF or Texas Instruments, note the vote and margin. If it's something else, it's background.
- **Any mention of Kaler attendance boundaries.** Meg's networks are asking directly. If a council member or parent raises the question during public comment and gets an answer, that's worth a follow-up post.
- **Document page numbers.** The council packet likely includes the school budget presentation as a PDF. If the city's budget documents are posted on the website before the meeting, Meg should download it and have it open on her iPad so she can cite page numbers when posting figures tonight.
